text: The video illustrates an extended left hepatic lobectomy combined with chordate lobectomy on block, performed for metastatic carcinoma to the liver.

CT scan shows a centrally situated tumor lying close to the vena cava which is indicated by the arrow. The tumor abuts the right side of the chordate process and obstructs the middle hepatic vein. This scan illustrates the position of the tumor invading and obstructing the middle hepatic vein.

The surgical field displays the left hepatic vein marked by a yellow arrow and the right hepatic vein marked by a blue arrow. The tumor is closely applied to the right hepatic vein, while there is greater clearance on the left side. A green arrow indicates an incidental hemangioma located at the tip of the left lateral segment.

An intraoperative ultrasound scan displays the tumor's close proximity to the left portal pedicle, marked by a yellow arrow, and its close approximation to the right hepatic vein, suggesting the need for an extended right hepatic lobectomy.
The surgeon exposes, dissects, and isolates the left portal vein. The left portal vein is then divided below the take-off of the chordate vein using an endogia vascular stapler.

The surgeon encircles the left hepatic duct with a suture for later control. The line of demarcation separating segments two, three, and four of the left liver from segments five and eight of the anterior sector of the right liver is visible and marked.
